entry.title: Visiting Finland as a Student
entry.content:
Visiting Finland as a Student: My Experience

If you are a student, you know exactly how challenging it can be to plan a trip on a budget. In this blog, I’m going to share my experience traveling to Finland and help you decide if it should be your next destination.

Finland is an amazing country with incredible sights, though most people tend to worry about two things: the weather and the transportation.

Getting There: The Scenic Route

My journey began by exploring Estonia first. While the two countries aren’t land neighbors, they are very close. Instead of flying, I took the ferry across the Gulf of Finland.

If you usually travel by bus or plane, you know how exhausting those trips can be. Going by ferry is one of those rare travel experiences that doesn’t feel tiring because the journey itself is part of the adventure—especially if you haven’t spent much time at sea. For me, it was a fantastic experience!

Navigating Finland

When you arrive at the port (the specific one depends on your ferry company), you’ll need to head into the city. If you arrive late like I did, having your transportation sorted is a lifesaver.

The App: The official transportation app is HSL, available on both the App Store and Google Play.

The Ticket: I highly recommend buying “Day Tickets” if you are staying for more than 24 hours. It’s much more cost-effective than buying single tickets, and it gives you the freedom to reach all the major tourist spots.

The Zones: When buying a ticket, the app will ask you to select zones (ranging from A to D). You can extend your area of eligibility based on where you plan to go, and the app makes it very clear which zones you’ll need.

Top Places to Visit

I categorize Finnish travel into three types: Island visits, Arctic visits, and City visits.

While I didn’t make it to the Arctic this time—which I’m still a little sad about—if you have the chance, you should absolutely visit Lapland!

Helsinki and the Islands

A visit to Helsinki is non-negotiable. If you have your daily HSL ticket, I suggest using the tramway to get around. Save your energy for the islands, because you’ll be doing a lot of walking there (and it’s worth every step!).

Helsinki is a remarkable city with deep history. Here is a pro-tip: that HSL ticket you bought? It actually includes the ferry from Helsinki to the nearby islands! It was a pleasant shock to realize our standard transit pass covered a cruise. The boats are in great shape and the views as you pass through the archipelago are insane.

Note: Even though the islands are historic sites, people do live there, so be mindful of the local neighborhoods!

Happy traveling!
entry.summary: Do you want to visit Finland but you are unsure about the transportations and places to visit? Learn from my experience!

entry.title: What to use on macOS for containers? w/OrbStack

link.title: What to use on macOS for containers? w/OrbStack

entry.content:
Running Docker & Kubernetes on macOS: Why OrbStack Changes Everything

Do you want to run Docker and Kubernetes on macOS but aren’t sure which tech stack to choose? Then you’re in the right place. I’m not going to walk you through a step-by-step setup; instead, I’ll focus on the benefits of picking the right tool and the overall developer experience.

I’ve developed on Linux, Windows, and currently use macOS as my main daily driver. The caveat with Docker is that it’s inherently Linux-native, which means it can be incredibly heavy on other operating systems. On top of that, it gets pretty messy trying to maintain all your containers, volumes, images, and the overall structure of your Kubernetes clusters. If you’ve used it for a while, you already know how fast dangling containers and failed images can eat up your storage space in no time.

Licensing Note: If you’re working on open-source or personal projects, OrbStack is completely free. However, if you plan to use it for commercial business projects, you’ll need a paid subscription. Check out their pricing page for details.

The Problem with Local Containers

Running containers locally can get incredibly resource-intensive. Spinning up a single, lightweight container might give you the illusion of an optimized engine, but the moment you start building complex microservices, the cracks start to show.

This is where OrbStack comes in. If you are using an Apple Silicon (M-series) Mac, OrbStack is supported natively and runs like a dream. It handles your Docker containers, runs a lightweight Kubernetes cluster, and even lets you spin up full Linux virtual machines if you need them.

The moment I discovered OrbStack, managing my cloud-native projects became significantly easier. Even though I have 24GB of RAM on my machine, it is always better to use highly optimized tools that don’t hog your system resources. And if you are working with less RAM than that, switching to a lightweight engine like this is basically a necessity.

Blazing Boot Times

Let’s talk about boot time first. If you’ve ever waited for traditional Docker Desktop to start up, you know it’s the perfect excuse to go grab a coffee. OrbStack, on the other hand, boots in about two seconds — practically instantaneous.

When you are spinning up an entire architecture of microservices — like a custom application or a complex DevOps platform with its own databases and API gateways — you don’t want your laptop spinning its fans like a jet engine. Because OrbStack is natively optimized for Apple Silicon:

  • CPU usage stays close to zero when idle
  • Your Mac won’t break a sweat
  • You won’t be burning through your battery life just to keep background containers alive

Kubernetes & Networking — Out of the Box

Then there is the Kubernetes and networking side of things. If you are grinding through heavy hands-on labs, maybe studying for admin certifications, or trying to simulate complex environments locally, you need a cluster that actually cooperates.

OrbStack gives you a lightweight Kubernetes cluster right out of the box, but the real game-changer is how it handles the network.

It automatically assigns local domain names to your containers. This means you can just hit something like app.orb.local in your browser without ever having to touch your /etc/hosts file.

Imagine trying to containerize and prototype a heavy, multi-layered system — like a 5G core network — directly on your Mac. With legacy tools, configuring the IP routing and port forwarding between all those different components is an absolute nightmare. With OrbStack, bidirectional networking just works out of the box, letting your host machine and your containers communicate effortlessly.

Final Thoughts

If you’re building anything cloud-native on an M-series Mac, you really owe it to yourself to give OrbStack a try. The whole point of a local environment is to make your life easier.

Instead of fighting with bloated software and constantly managing your system resources, you get an engine that just quietly does its job in the background. It takes the headache out of local development and makes building microservices fun again.

Give it a spin, watch how much smoother your containers run, and let me know how it changes your workflow.

Happy building!

You can reach me out on:

Connect with me
entry.summary: Did you switch to macOS recently? Or you want to manage your containers in your mac computer? You are in the right blog.

entry.title: About Me
entry.content:
About Me

My name is Berk Ünsal, and I am currently specializing in the cloud and networking areas of computer science. I am studying at Ostim Technical University and completed my Erasmus at the Latvia University of Life Sciences and Technologies.

Why I Built This Site

The reason I made this site is for people to learn and engage through my experiences. I will be sharing my journey on:

  • Making technical projects
  • Traveling and studying abroad
  • And whatever else comes to my mind actually!

Under the Hood

This site is proudly hosted through GitHub Pages, so feel free to check out the source code on my GitHub and even take it as a template for your own blog page :)
